summary: I was born in Djibouti and grew up there surrounded by African culture. Even though I lived in Africa, I was familiar with American culture and dreamed of coming to the United States. Djibouti is where my roots are, and it shaped who I am before I immigrated in 1996.

I immigrated to the United States in 1996 because I was drawn to the American Dream and the opportunities it offered. Growing up, I was familiar with American culture and wanted to be part of it while also giving back to Africa. The United States became my new home where I could pursue my goals and contribute to the community.

Since 1997, Minnesota has been my second home and a welcoming place for Somalis like me. I chose to live here because the Somali community felt accepted and supported more than in other states. Minnesota is where I started my business and became deeply involved in community activism.

I founded Afro Deli in October 2010 in the Cedar-Riverside neighborhood to share East African food with Minnesotans. Afro Deli represents the African community and serves as a place where anyone, regardless of background, can come together. We also support African development by donating part of our profits to a nonprofit, which makes me proud to contribute socially through this venture.

Cedar-Riverside in Minneapolis is where I opened the first Afro Deli location, also known as the West Bank. This neighborhood is a vibrant hub for the African community and a perfect place to introduce African cuisine to a diverse audience. It’s been a blessing to start my restaurant here and be part of this multicultural area.

In 2015, I expanded Afro Deli by opening a second location in St. Paul, Minnesota. This allowed us to reach more people and continue sharing East African food and culture beyond Minneapolis. St. Paul became an important part of our growth and community engagement efforts.
